title: Dyeing with purple loosestrife
subtitle: An olive-green to warm tan dye from purple loosestrife flowers on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Despite its name and vivid magenta flowers, purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria) does not give purple on wool. It produces a range of warm olive-greens, tans, and muted golds depending on the mordant. This entry covers harvesting, mordanting with alum, and dyeing a 100 g skein to an olive-tan colour.
sourceNotes: Purple loosestrife dyeing synthesised from general natural dye references. Lythrum salicaria is a British wetland plant whose dye properties are documented in UK natural dyeing literature. The colour is not purple: tannin and flavonoid compounds produce olive-gold rather than the red-purple of the flower pigments.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt used to bond natural dye permanently to fibre. Potassium alum is the standard mordant for protein fibres.
The dry weight of the fibre to be dyed, used as the reference for calculating mordant and plant material quantities. Abbreviated WOF.

A naturally occurring compound found in many plant dye sources that bonds to fibre and contributes tan, brown, and olive tones. Purple loosestrife is relatively tannin-rich, contributing to its olive-gold dye colour.
The strained liquid obtained by simmering the plant material, into which the mordanted fibre is placed to take up colour.

attrs.title: Invasive species note
attrs.body: Purple loosestrife is invasive in some regions outside the UK. Do not plant it if you gather it from the wild. In the UK it is native and picking the flowers does not spread it.

content.text: Purple loosestrife is one of the most strikingly coloured wildflowers in British wetlands, but its magenta flower pigments are water-soluble anthocyanins that do not bind to wool and fade rapidly. The dye colour from a
and flavonoid compounds, producing a warm olive-tan that pairs well with woad blue or weld yellow. The
is easy to manage and suitable for beginners. Use 200%
of fresh plant material for a good depth of colour.

items.name: Fresh purple loosestrife, flowers and upper stems
Stainless steel dye pot, 6 litre capacity

content.text: Dissolve potassium alum in hot water and add to the pot with enough water to submerge the skein. Add the pre-wetted wool. Raise the temperature slowly to 85°C over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently. Allow to cool in the mordant liquid. The mordanted skein can be used straight away or kept damp in a sealed bag in the refrigerator for up to two days.

Harvest the flowers and upper stems of purple loosestrife at peak bloom (July to August in the UK). Pack the plant material loosely into the pot with water to cover. Bring to a simmer and hold at 85°C for 45-60 minutes. The water will turn from pink to a warm amber-orange as the anthocyanins cook off and the tannin compounds release. Strain the plant material out. The strained bath will be warm amber-brown, not pink.

Add the mordanted skein to the strained dye bath. Heat slowly to 80-85°C over 20 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. The wool will take on a warm olive-tan to golden-brown colour depending on the plant material age and the hardness of the water. Allow the skein to cool completely in the bath to deepen the take-up, then rinse in warm water and squeeze gently.

Rinse in two or three changes of warm water until the water runs clear. Hang to dry in the shade. The dry colour will be lighter and slightly more muted than the wet colour: the olive-gold reads more clearly on the dry skein than in the dye pot. Record the result with a small swatch in a dye journal.

items.problem: The finished colour is pinkish rather than olive-gold
items.cause: The dye bath temperature was too low and the anthocyanin pigments did not cook off; only the fugitive pink pigments were taken up
items.fix: Ensure the dye bath reaches and holds at 85°C for the full 45-60 minutes before adding the fibre. At this temperature the anthocyanins degrade and the stable tannin colour dominates.

items.problem: The colour is very pale on the finished skein
items.cause: The plant material was past peak bloom and had lower pigment concentration
items.fix: Gather loosestrife when the flowering spike is at least half in flower and half still in bud. Use a higher plant ratio: 300% WOF of fresh material for a deeper result.

items.problem: The colour fades after one wash
items.cause: The alum mordant was not fully fixed before dyeing
items.fix: Repeat the mordant step with a fresh alum bath and re-dye in a fresh dye bath. Check that the alum was fully dissolved before adding the fibre and that the temperature held at 85°C for the full 45 minutes.
